title: Zaalouk
subtitle: Moroccan smoked aubergine and tomato salad
excerpt: Zaalouk is a Moroccan cooked salad: aubergine and tomatoes softened together in olive oil with garlic, cumin, and paprika until the whole thing collapses into a rough, deeply flavoured dip. It is eaten warm or at room temperature with bread, and gets better as it cools.
sourceNotes:
• Paula Wolfert, *Couscous and Other Good Food from Morocco* (1973). Principal reference for the cooked-salad technique and the cumin-paprika spice base.
• Claudia Roden, *The Food of Morocco* (2008). Cross-reference for the aubergine-to-tomato ratio and the finishing with fresh herbs.

recipe.batchNotes: The recipe doubles easily and keeps in the fridge for 3 days. Makes an excellent accompaniment to multiple dishes over several meals.
recipe.makeAheadNotes: Zaalouk improves as it sits. Make up to 2 days ahead and refrigerate. Bring to room temperature before serving.

content.text: Zaalouk is one of a family of Moroccan cooked vegetable salads served as part of the opening spread at a meal. The aubergine is not roasted separately — it goes straight into the pan with the tomatoes and cooks down together, so the two vegetables become indistinguishable in texture. The result is dip-like but chunkier than hummus, meant for scooping with bread rather than spreading.

The dish is better at room temperature than hot, and better still the next day. The olive oil comes through more clearly when the salad has had time to cool, and the cumin deepens. Make it a few hours ahead if possible.
A wide frying pan gives the aubergine space to cook down without steaming in a pile. If the pan is too small, the aubergine will sweat rather than soften evenly.

Heat {{olive-oil}} of olive oil in a wide frying pan over a medium heat. Add {{aubergine}} of cubed aubergine and season with salt. Cook for 10 to 12 minutes, stirring occasionally, until the cubes have softened and are starting to colour. Add {{garlic}} of garlic, {{cumin-ground}} of ground cumin, {{paprika-sweet}} of sweet paprika, and {{chilli-flakes}} of chilli flakes if using. Stir for 1 minute.

Add {{tinned-tomatoes}} of tinned tomatoes and stir to combine. Simmer over a medium heat for 20 minutes, stirring every few minutes and pressing the aubergine pieces gently with the back of the spoon, until the mixture is thick and the aubergine has fully collapsed. It should look almost like a rough paste — not dry, but holding its shape without pooling liquid.

Remove from the heat. Stir in the juice of {{lemon}} and half of the coriander and parsley. Taste for seasoning and adjust. Transfer to a serving plate or bowl and scatter the remaining herbs over the top. Serve warm or at room temperature with flatbread or pitta.

items.symptom: Aubergine is dry and not softening
items.cause: Not enough oil, or the heat was too high and the surface dried before the interior cooked.
items.fix: Aubergine absorbs oil heavily at first. Add a splash more olive oil and reduce the heat slightly. A little more oil is better than a dry, fibrous result.

items.symptom: Mixture is watery rather than thick
items.cause: The tomatoes were not reduced enough.
items.fix: Increase the heat slightly and stir frequently until the liquid evaporates and the mixture collapses to a thick paste. The final texture should hold a spoon trail.

items.cause: Not enough salt, or the lemon was not added.
items.fix: Season generously; aubergine absorbs salt heavily, and do not skip the lemon juice. A squeeze at the end makes a visible difference to the brightness of the dish.

content.text: Zaalouk is one of several Moroccan cooked salads served at the start of a meal alongside bread, olives, and preserved lemons. Similar aubergine-and-tomato preparations appear across North Africa and the Middle East under different names and with different spicing. The Moroccan version is defined by the cumin and paprika combination and the generous olive oil — it should taste of both vegetables and be clearly fragrant with spice. In Britain it is less well known than hummus or baba ganoush but has the same function: a dip to begin a meal, simple to make, better when made ahead.
